english: tse 'pos kyaṅ blao (tshe 'phos kyang blao) Dzl. then I will rather die; less frq. with na: śi-na yaṅ blai (shi na yang blai) since even death is to be preferred Dzl.; frq. it may be rendered by 'may', rgyál-bar gyur kyaṅ blao (rgyal bar gyur kyang blao) then may rather . . . gain the victory (than that I should . . .) Dzl.; also pleon.: kyoḍ mig-gis mi mtoṅ yaṅ blai (khyod mig gis mi mthong yang blai) be it that you do not see it (it is of no consequence whether you see or not) Dzl. 2, 7. In the passage Tar. 123, 8 bla (bla) seems to stand as an adv. for 'very', Schf. — Comp. bla-gáb (bla gab), bla-gós (bla gos) (W. vulgo *tsá-dar, tsá-sar*) = gzán-gos (gzan gos), upper garment, cloth, serving Indians, and occasionally also Tibetans as a covering, = toga, ἱμάτιον; bla-gáb prág-pa gcig-tu gzár-ba (bla gab phrag pa gcig tu gzar ba) to throw the toga over one shoulder, frq.; bla-gab méd-pa (bla gab med pa), 1. without upper garment Dzl. 2. having no wish, no desire, free from passion(?) — bla-bré (bla bre), also bla-re (bla re), canopy, dais Dzl. and elsewh. — blá-ma (bla ma) उत्तर 1. the higher, upper, superior; blá-mar byéd-pa (bla mar byed pa) to esteem highly, to honour, syn. to bkúr-sti byéd-pa (bkur sti byed pa) Domañ, Tar.; the exact grammatical explanation of mii blá-mai čós-kyi čo-'prúl (mii bla mai chos kyi cho 'phrul) Dzl. V, or of the similar passage mii čos blá-mai rdzu-'prúl (mii chos bla mai rdzu 'phrul) Burn. I, 164, offers some difficulties, although it is evident, that Burn. has hit the sense better than Sch. Of later date is the signification: 2. the superior, i.e. spiritual teacher, father confessor, गुरु, with the genit. of the person Pth.; in a more gen. sense: ecclesiastic, priest, 'Lama' Thgr., Pth.; in East. Tib. a title designing a high eccles. degree, something like 'D.D.' v. Desg. 247, 371; bla-mčóḍ (bla mchod) for blá-ma daṅ mčóḍ-gnás (bla ma dang mchod gnas) ecclesiastic and sacrificing priest, whether it be one and the same person, or two different individuals Pth., Mil.; bla-(ma-)čéṅ(-po) (bla (ma) chen (po)) chief Lama, Grand-Lama. — bla-braṅ (bla brang) resp. for dwelling-room or house of a Lama or Lamas, whilst gzim-káṅ (gzim khang), po-braṅ (pho brang) are the resp. expressions for secular dignitaries. — bla-slób (bla slob), blá-ma daṅ slób-ma (bla ma dang slob ma), the Lama and his disciple Sch. — smán-gyi-bla (sman gyi bla) v. sman (sman). — II. Sch. 'soul, life'; acc. to oral explanations: 1. strength, power, vitality, e.g. in food, scents etc., just like bčuḍ (bcud). — 2. blessing, power of blessings, like gyaṅ (g.yang), e.g. *ṭim-mé mi-la la čém-po mi dug = yaṅ mi čag* (*khrims med mi la la chem po mi dug = yang mi chag*) C., no blessing attends a contemner of the law. — 3. an object with which a person's life is ominously connected; thus very commonly bla-śiṅ (bla shing) a tree of fate (gen. a juniper or in W. a willow-tree, ral-lcáṅ (ral lcang)), planted at a child's birth; rgyál-poi bla-gyú (rgyal poi bla g.yu) the king's turkois of life Glr.; bla-dár (bla dar) a little flag on the house-top, on which benedictions are written; bla gnás (bla gnas) the omen is lasting, propitious, nyams (nyams) it is vanishing, foreboding danger; so prob. also Dzl. 20, 17, where it is not at once equivalent to 'soul' (Sch.). — III. frq. incorr. for sla (sla). — IV. in some combinations it has a signification not yet accounted for, e.g. bla rdól-ba (bla rdol ba) Sch. to find fault with, to blame, abuse, without a reason; bla-tse (bla tse)(?) Lex.
v. blá-ma (bla ma) sub bla I. (bla I.)
Gram.; Sch.: 'a primitive word, an abstract noun'.(?)
Sch.: 'well! that may be! so much the better!'
v. blá-ma (bla ma) sub bla I. (bla I.)
, བླ་ཤིང་ bla-śiṅ (bla shing) v. bla II. (bla II.)
v. bla-bré (bla bre) sub bla I. (bla I.)
1. sub bde-blág (bde blag) q.v. — 2. sub btso-blag (btso blag) q.v.

1. pf. blags (blags), rná-ba blág-pa (rna ba blag pa) = rná-ba gtáḍ-pa (rna ba gtad pa) Lex.: to incline one's ear to, to lend one's ear, to listen to (blág-pa (blag pa) not by itself 'to hear' ('s.)) —
